title: Automatic Discovery of Disease Subgroups by Contrasting with Healthy Controls
abstract: In biomedical Subgroup Discovery, practitioners are interested in discovering interpretable and homogeneous subgroups within a group of patients. In this paper, assuming that healthy subjects (i.e., controls) share common but irrelevant factors of variation with the patients, we motivate and develop a Contrastive Subgroup Discovery method, entitled Deep UCSL. By contrasting patients with controls, Deep UCSL identifies subgroups driven solely by pathological factors, ignoring common variability shared with healthy subjects. Our framework employs a deep feature extractor to learn a discriminative representation space. Mathematically, we derive a novel loss based on the conditional joint likelihood of latent clusters and patient/control labels, optimized via an Expectation-Maximization strategy alternating between subgroup inference and feature encoder updates. A regularization term further encourages representations to capture disease-specific variability while ignoring variability shared with controls. Compared to previous related works, our approach quantitatively improves the quality of the estimated subgroups, as demonstrated on a MNIST example and four distinct real medical imaging datasets. Code and datasets are available at: https://github.com/rlouiset/deep_ucsl.

paper_title: Automatic Discovery of Disease Subgroups by Contrasting with Healthy Controls

objective.answer: The primary objective of the paper is to develop a Contrastive Subgroup Discovery method, called Deep UCSL, to identify disease-specific subgroups by contrasting patients with healthy controls, focusing on pathological factors while ignoring common variability shared with healthy subjects.
objective.evidence: In this paper, assuming that healthy subjects (i.e., controls) share common but irrelevant factors of variation with the patients, we motivate and develop a Contrastive Subgroup Discovery method, entitled Deep UCSL.

knowledge_gap.answer: Existing subgroup discovery methods either rely on user-defined features and linear predictors or are not generalizable to other domains, limiting their effectiveness in identifying disease-specific subgroups.
knowledge_gap.evidence: To sum up, actual subgroup discovery works are either not generalizable to other domains or depend on user-defined feature choices and generally do not leverage the representation quality of non-linear deep learning methods.

answer: Deep UCSL is the first deep learning method for Subgroup Discovery that performs disease/healthy classification while identifying subgroups in the diseased class.
The method introduces a clustering regularization loss that forces the learned representation to disregard healthy population variability, focusing only on disease-specific variability.
The paper proposes a novel Expectation-Maximization optimization process that alternates between subgroup inference and feature encoder updates.
evidence: To the best of our knowledge, we propose the first Deep Learning method for Subgroup Discovery that performs disease/healthy classification while identifying subgroups in the diseased class.
We motivate and design a clustering regularization loss that forces the learned representation to disregard the healthy population variability focusing only on the disease-specific variability.

Then, as in UCSL [21], we propose the use of an Expectation-Maximization optimization process to alternate between the conditional joint likelihood parameters update and the subgroups pseudo-labels estimation.

inspirational_papers.answer:
- Caron et al. (2018) Deep clustering for unsupervised learning of visual features. (Methodological precursors)
- Varol et al. (2017) HYDRA: Revealing heterogeneity of imaging and genetic patterns through a multiple max-margin discriminative analysis framework. (Papers with limitations addressed by this work)
- Louiset et al. (2021) UCSL: A machine learning expectation-maximization framework for unsupervised clustering driven by supervised learning. (Methodological precursors)
inspirational_papers.evidence: Notably, emerging machine learning methods, such as HYDRA [20] and our preliminary work UCSL [21], have proposed a framework for Subgroup Discovery, where the supervised classification (healthy vs. diseased samples) and the subgroup identification (clustering of diseased samples only) depends on each other.

steps.step: Train a deep feature extractor to produce representations for identifying disease-specific subgroups.
steps.input: Dataset with known labels (0: healthy, 1: disease)
steps.evidence: Our framework employs a deep feature extractor to learn a discriminative representation space.

steps.step: Estimate subgroup pseudo-labels using a regularized K-means clustering on positive samples.
steps.evidence: We first compute pθ(ci = k|xi, yi = +1) using a regularized K-mean.

steps.step: Optimize the model using an Expectation-Maximization strategy.
steps.evidence: The optimization procedure consists of alternatively 1) estimating the subgroups pseudo-labels and 2) updating the features encoder.

tools.evidence: We use a Deep Neural Network as an automatic feature extractor to produce representations suitable for identifying relevant disease subgroups.

tools.name: K-Means
tools.description: Used for estimating subgroup pseudo-labels during the Expectation step.
tools.evidence: We first compute pθ(ci = k|xi, yi = +1) using a regularized K-mean.

benchmark_datasets.usage: Used to demonstrate the differences between Deep Clustering and Subtype Discovery.

evaluation_metrics.name: Class Balanced Accuracy
evaluation_metrics.purpose: Measures the balanced accuracy of binary classification between true labels (healthy: 0, disease: 1) and predicted diagnoses.
evaluation_metrics.application: Used to evaluate the performance of the classification task.
evaluation_metrics.evidence: Class Balanced Accuracy (Class B-ACC): Measures the balanced accuracy of binary classification between true labels (healthy: 0, disease: 1) and predicted diagnoses p(yj|xj).

evaluation_metrics.name: Subgroup Balanced Accuracy
evaluation_metrics.purpose: Evaluates the balanced accuracy between inferred pathological subgroups compared to the true subgroups.
evaluation_metrics.application: Computed on disease samples only to evaluate subgroup identification.
evaluation_metrics.evidence: Subgroup Balanced Accuracy (Subgroup B-ACC): Evaluates the balanced accuracy between inferred pathological subgroups compared to the true subgroups cj (not used during training, only during evaluation).

limitations.name: Limited Generalizability
limitations.description: The method's performance may be sensitive to the choice of input features and dataset characteristics.
limitations.evidence: UCSL performance is highly sensitive to the choice of input features: it degrades when using generic VAE latent representations but improves with carefully engineered domain-specific features.

future_directions.name: Generalize to Other Modalities
future_directions.description: Extend the method to work with MRI and CT scans beyond X-ray images.
future_directions.evidence: In future work, we plan to evaluate our pipeline on multimodal medical imaging datasets.
